title: Procedure
text:
← Back to Lab 2 overview
The lab collects six step-response datasets — three parameter configurations (Baseline, Feedforward, Tuned) × two control channels (speed, yaw-rate)% CLAUDE: Change to a bullet point list. The same field workflow runs three times; only the parameter set changes between cases. Quality is checked at the field before each parameter-set move, so problems are caught while the boat is still in the water.

title: Procedure
text:
1. Pre-lab preparation
% CLAUDE: We need two things here. Day before and day-of prep. The former is to make sure you have your boat, 900 MHz radio, and R/C transmitter and ground laptop ready to go.
- Team roles — planner (Mission Planner operator) scribe and pilot. - Ground laptop ready — Mission Planner installed and connected to the radio. - Parameter files staged — three .param files corresponding to experiments A / B / C, located under utils/ardu_params/. % CLAUDE: They are only given the baseline .param file; they have to edit it to create the other two. - Vehicle ready — battery charged, props/rudder clear, RC bound, GPS lock confirmed. - Test card / data sheet — one row per dataset (six total) for noting parameter values, observations, and quality-check verdict.

title: Procedure
text:
2. Field setup
Steps to confirm at the launch site, before any data collection.

Launch and recover the USV safely; clear of swimmers and obstructions.
Establish telemetry link (Mission Planner connected at expected baud).
Confirm flight mode is ACRO.
Confirm logging is enabled and a new log file starts on each arm (LOG_FILE_DSRMROT = 1).
Trim and zero check on RC sticks (no commanded motion at center stick).

title: Procedure
text:
3. Experiment execution
For each of the three experiments (A → B → C), repeat the same field workflow:

Load the parameter set for this experiment via Mission Planner (Config → Full Parameter Tree → Load from file).
Verify the relevant ATC_* parameters at the vehicle (Mission Planner shows live values).
Speed step responses — see §4.1 for stick technique.
Yaw-rate step responses — see §4.2.
Field quality check — pull the log, plot the candidate steps, decide keep / repeat (§5).
Save and label — note the log filename and parameter values on the test card.









Experiment
Parameter changes from previous
Notes




A — Baseline
Lab preset configuration; ArduRover stock PID gains; FF off; rate limiters off; throttle baseline off
First case — fully load the lab baseline .param


B — Feedforward
Restore ATC_SPEED_FF and ATC_STR_RAT_FF to lab values
Same PID gains as A


C — Tuned
Adjust ATC_SPEED_P/I/D and ATC_STR_RAT_P/I/D for best response
FF kept on

title: Procedure
text:
4. Step-input technique
Manual step inputs from the RC stick, with quiet hold periods on either side of each step so the response is clean.

4.1 Speed (throttle) steps

TBD — magnitudes (e.g. 50%, 75% stick), hold time before/after, number of repeats per dataset.
TBD — open-water distance required for each run.



4.2 Yaw-rate (steering) steps

TBD — magnitudes (left, right, full deflection), hold time, number of repeats.
TBD — pre-step heading and forward speed (does the boat need to be moving?).



4.3 Common gotchas

TBD — wind / current effect on speed steps
TBD — saturation behavior (full stick → control effort clipping)
TBD — minimum inter-step gap for the response to settle

title: Procedure
text:
5. Quality check (before leaving the field)
Run the visual inspection on the laptop while the boat is still launched, so any failed dataset can be recollected on the spot.

Pull the latest .BIN log from the autopilot SD card or via Mission Planner.
Run bin2mat.py to convert: produces a .mat file alongside the .BIN.
Run proc_closedloop_response.m in MATLAB to plot the timeseries.
Check each step in the figure:

Setpoint reaches the commanded value (no rate-limited ramp instead of a step)
Measured response settles to a steady value within ~10 s
Control effort is not pinned at ±1 for an extended period (saturation)
PIDT / PIDS log channels are present (PID-internal logging is on)
No long gaps in the GPS or STER sample stream


If any check fails for the dataset just collected → recollect before moving on. If it’s a parameter or vehicle issue → resolve, then continue.

title: Procedure
text:
6. End-of-day
Wrap up the field session.

All six .mat files transferred to the team’s shared location.
Test card filled in (one row per dataset, with quality-check verdict).
Vehicle returned to nominal parameter configuration (rate limiters restored, FF restored, throttle baseline restored) — load a .param file with the post-lab nominal values.
Battery on charger, vehicle stowed.

title: Procedure
text:
7. Hand-off to next week
Datasets and notes are the input to the Week-8 analysis: process each .mat with closedloop_stepinfo.m to extract rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error, then compare across the three experiments.

title: Autopilot
text:
Documentation

Stabilization layer — speed and yaw-rate PID — design and implementation of the lowest-level control loops, with full parameter and log-channel reference.
ArduPilot doc references — curated entry points into the upstream ArduPilot documentation (tuning, parameters, log analysis).

title: Autopilot
text:
Parameters
The vehicle parameter snapshots (.param files) and per-lab baseline configurations live in utils/ardu_params/. See the README there for which file corresponds to which lab and how to load it via Mission Planner.

title: Autopilot
text:
Log Processing Pipeline
Field-collected ArduPilot .BIN logs are processed in two stages before MATLAB analysis:

.BIN → .mat — utils/ardu_utils/bin2mat.py extracts every numeric field from every message type into a MATLAB .mat file. Field names follow {MSGTYPE}_{field} (e.g. GPS_Spd, RCOU_C3, PIDS_Tar). Logged ArduPilot parameters are saved as a params struct.
MATLAB analysis — per-lab scripts under book/wXX_*/code/ load the .mat and produce timeseries and metrics.

title: Stabilization Layer — Speed and Yaw-Rate PID
text:
← Back to Autopilot
The stabilization layer is the lowest level of ArduRover’s control hierarchy. Two independent PID loops — one for speed (throttle channel), one for yaw rate (steering channel) — translate the pilot’s high-level commands into actuator outputs. Higher-level autonomous modes (HOLD, LOITER, AUTO) wrap additional outer loops around these, but in ACRO mode the pilot’s stick deflections become the setpoints to the stabilization PIDs directly. That makes ACRO the right mode for closed-loop step-response testing.

title: Stabilization Layer — Speed and Yaw-Rate PID
text:
Architecture






Diagram under construction. Source: site/autopilot/images/usv_stabilization_arch.drawio (edit in diagrams.net or the VS Code “Draw.io Integration” extension). After editing, re-export the SVG with drawio --export --format svg --output site/autopilot/images/usv_stabilization_arch.svg site/autopilot/images/usv_stabilization_arch.drawio.






Speed and yaw-rate stabilization loops


Both loops share the same structure:

Stick → setpoint scaling (CRUISE_SPEED for throttle, ACRO_TURN_RATE for steering)
Acceleration limiter on the commanded setpoint (ATC_ACCEL_MAX / ATC_STR_ACC_MAX)
Target low-pass filter (*_FLTT) — softens setpoint steps before they enter the loop
Error junction subtracting the measured value from the (filtered) setpoint
Error low-pass filter (*_FLTE) — attenuates measurement noise on the error
PID controller with separate P, I, D, and FF terms
Output sum — speed loop adds an additional CRUISE_THROTTLE baseline; yaw loop does not

For closed-loop step-response experiments, several of these features are intentionally disabled (acceleration limiters off, throttle baseline off, FF off) so that the response captures the PID dynamics alone. See the per-channel quick references below.

title: Stabilization Layer — Speed and Yaw-Rate PID
section: Logged channels — a clarification on naming
text:
Logged channels — a clarification on naming
The signal names referenced throughout this page (THR_DesSpeed, STER_TurnRate, RCOU_C3, PIDS_*, PIDT_*) are ArduPilot DataFlash log message types — the binary .BIN log format that ArduPilot writes to the SD card during a flight/run. They are not MAVLink message names, although several MAVLink messages carry overlapping data.

DataFlash log (BIN file) — onboard binary log, complete record of internal state. We extract these with bin2mat.py and analyze them in MATLAB.
MAVLink — telemetry/command protocol between vehicle and ground station (Mission Planner). Subset of the same data, sent over the radio link.

For the PID-tuning labs we use the DataFlash log: it has higher rate, more channels, and full PID-internal state via PIDT_* (speed) and PIDS_* (steering).

title: Stabilization Layer — Speed and Yaw-Rate PID
text:
Speed (Surge) Control

Design
Single-input, single-output PID + feedforward + baseline feedforward.



Loop element
Source / value




Setpoint
RC throttle stick ([−1, +1]) × CRUISE_SPEED (m/s)


Measurement
GPS_Spd — GPS ground speed (m/s, ~5 Hz)


Output
Throttle command, normalized [−1, +1] → PWM via RCOU_C3


Plant
USV surge dynamics (thruster + hull drag)



Why these inputs/outputs make sense for the marine vehicle:

GPS speed as feedback — boats lack a wheel encoder, and the body-frame velocity from the IMU drifts. GPS gives a clean, slow ground-truth measurement. The 5 Hz update rate is one of the dominant time constants in this loop and shapes what kinds of dynamics the PID can possibly track.
Reversible throttle — with ATC_BRAKE = 1, the controller can command negative motor output to actively decelerate. Without it, deceleration relies entirely on hydrodynamic drag.



Parameters — ATC_SPEED_* group
PID gains and filter cutoffs:










Parameter
Description
Default
Vehicle
Units




ATC_SPEED_P
Proportional gain
0.20
1.0
(motor)/(m/s)


ATC_SPEED_I
Integral gain — drives steady-state error to zero
0.20
0.2
(motor)/(m/s·s)


ATC_SPEED_D
Derivative gain — rarely useful (GPS-noisy)
0.00
0
(motor·s)/(m/s)


ATC_SPEED_FF
Feed-forward, output ∝ setpoint
0.0
0
(motor)/(m/s)


ATC_SPEED_IMAX
Anti-windup clamp on I-term contribution
1.00
1.0
normalized


ATC_SPEED_FLTT
Low-pass cutoff on target
0 (off)
0
Hz


ATC_SPEED_FLTE
Low-pass cutoff on error
10

ATC_SPEED_FLTD

0 (off)
0

ATC_SPEED_SMAX
Slew-rate limit on PID output (anti-oscillation)
0 (disabled)

—

—

—

DataFlash log channels








Channel
Description
Units

Speed setpoint (after input filter)
m/s


GPS_Spd

m/s

PWM

m/s

m/s

normalized



THR_DesSpeed and PIDT_Tar differ only when an upstream filter or rate limiter is active. With the lab settings (acceleration limiters disabled, FLTT off), the two are essentially identical.


Step-response setup for this lab








Setting
Lab value
Why




ATC_ACCEL_MAX
0
Disable rate limiter — let the setpoint be a true step


CRUISE_THROTTLE
0

ATC_SPEED_FF
0 for baseline
FF restored in experiment B; tuned with PID in experiment C

title: Stabilization Layer — Speed and Yaw-Rate PID
text:
Yaw-Rate (Steering) Control

Design
Single-input, single-output PID + feedforward. Same structure as the speed loop, with rudder/skid-steer command in place of throttle.







Loop element
Source / value




Setpoint
RC steering stick ([−1, +1]) × ACRO_TURN_RATE (deg/s, internally rad/s)


Measurement
STER_TurnRate — body-frame yaw rate from EKF (rad/s)


Output
Steering command, normalized [−1, +1] → PWM via RCOU_C1


Plant
USV yaw dynamics (rudder + hull)



Why these matter for the marine vehicle:

EKF yaw rate as feedback — the IMU gyro provides a fast, accurate yaw rate measurement; the EKF (STER_*) blends gyro with magnetometer/GPS heading to suppress bias drift. Yaw rate updates much faster than the GPS speed signal in the surge loop, so the bandwidth ceiling of the yaw loop is set by vehicle dynamics, not measurement.
Strong feedforward by default — for a single-rudder boat, the steady rudder needed to hold a given yaw rate is approximately linear in the desired rate. The vehicle ships with ATC_STR_RAT_FF = 2.0 (10× the source default), reflecting that this contributes the majority of the open-loop control authority.



Cascaded heading controller (not in ACRO)
In autonomous modes (HOLD, LOITER, AUTO), an outer-loop heading controller (ATC_STR_ANG_P) wraps the yaw-rate loop, converting heading error to a desired yaw rate. ACRO bypasses that outer loop — the pilot stick commands yaw rate directly, so we tune only the inner rate loop in this lab.


Parameters — ATC_STR_RAT_* group
PID gains and filter cutoffs:










Parameter
Description
Default
Vehicle
Units




ATC_STR_RAT_P
Proportional gain
0.20
0.2
(motor)/(rad/s)


ATC_STR_RAT_I
Integral gain — eliminates steady-state offset
0.20
0.2
(motor)/(rad/s·s)


ATC_STR_RAT_D
Derivative gain — use sparingly, yaw rate is already a derivative of heading
0.00
0
(motor·s)/(rad/s)


ATC_STR_RAT_FF
Feed-forward, output ∝ desired rate
0.20
2.0
(motor)/(rad/s)


ATC_STR_RAT_IMAX

10

title: Stabilization Layer — Speed and Yaw-Rate PID
text:
Restoring normal operation
The lab disables several features (ATC_ACCEL_MAX = 0, ATC_STR_ACC_MAX = 0, ATC_STR_RAT_FF = 0, CRUISE_THROTTLE = 0) only to expose the closed-loop PID dynamics in step-response data. Restore these to their nominal values when the lab is finished — they’re useful in normal operation and required for the autonomous modes used in Lab 3.

title: Stabilization Layer — Speed and Yaw-Rate PID
text:

AR_AttitudeControl.cpp — parameter definitions and PID call sites
AC_PID.cpp — the underlying PID implementation that all _P, _I, _D, _FF, _FLT*, _IMAX, _SMAX, _PDMX parameters configure
Rover ACRO mode docs — official explanation of stick-to-rate mapping
Rover speed and steering tuning guide — official tuning recipe (different in tone from a controls-class treatment but useful for cross-reference)

title: Lab 2 — USV PID Tuning
text: Status: in progress through Week 7. This week is data collection; analysis and performance characterization happen in Week 8.

title: Lab 2 — USV PID Tuning
text:
Objectives
Tune the autopilot’s closed-loop control to maximize speed and yaw-rate tracking performance. By the end of this week each team has step-response data for three experiments × two channels = six datasets.








Experiment
What changes
What stays the same




A — Baseline
Lab preset configuration (rate limiters off, FF off, throttle baseline off); ArduRover stock PID gains
Other settings unchanged


B — Feedforward
Add feedforward gain to both speed and yaw-rate control loops. Gain values from our open-loop system identification lab.
Stock PID gains


C — Tuned
Tune PID gains for “best” response
Feedforward kept on



For each experiment, both the speed (surge/throttle) loop and the yaw-rate (steering/rudder) loop are exercised with manual step inputs, and the resulting closed-loop responses are logged and quality-checked.
This week’s deliverable: clean step-response logs for all six cases, with a brief quality-check note per dataset.
Next week: process the data with closedloop_stepinfo.m to quantify rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error — and use those metrics to compare the three experiments.

title: Lab 2 — USV PID Tuning
text:
Architecture overview
The lab tunes the two innermost feedback loops in ArduRover’s ACRO mode:

Speed (surge) loop — PID under ATC_SPEED_*, target from RC throttle scaled by CRUISE_SPEED, measurement from GPS.
Yaw-rate loop — PID under ATC_STR_RAT_*, target from RC steering scaled by ACRO_TURN_RATE, measurement from EKF/IMU.

For the block diagram, full parameter tables, and DataFlash log channel reference, see the centralized Autopilot → Stabilization Layer page. The lab-specific framing — which ATC_* parameters change between experiments A / B / C, and which stay fixed — is in §3 of the Procedure.

title: Lab 2 — USV PID Tuning
text:
Procedure overview
Working draft — see Procedure details.
At a high level:

Confirm preset parameters and the experiment-specific tuning state on the boat.
Run a series of throttle and steering step inputs in ACRO mode.
Pull the .BIN log, convert with bin2mat.py, and inspect with proc_closedloop_response.m.
Verify the data passes the quality check before returning to the dock.

title: Lab 2 — USV PID Tuning
section: End state — what “done” looks like this week
text:
End state — what “done” looks like this week
For each of the six datasets, you should be able to plot — using closedloop_stepinfo.m — a step-response figure that shows:

Setpoint (commanded step) overlaid on measured response
Control effort (normalized throttle or rudder) on a linked time axis
PID internals (PIDT_* for speed, PIDS_* for yaw rate) showing target, actual, P/I/D/FF terms, and total output

If those plots look clean — clear step, response settles, no clipping, no logging gaps — the dataset is good. If anything looks off, plan to recollect before leaving the field.

title: Lab 2 — USV PID Tuning
text:
Materials

Lab chapter PDF (build pending) — book/w07_lab_usv_pid/chapter/lab_usv_pid.pdf
ArduPilot Rover doc references — see ardupilot_references.md
MATLAB scripts — book/w07_lab_usv_pid/code/

proc_closedloop_response.m — visual inspection
closedloop_stepinfo.m — step-response metrics

Log conversion — bin2mat.py
